summary:
The Umayyad Emirate of Cordoba (756–929 AD) was founded by Abd al-Rahman I — the sole survivor of the [[event:2129|Umayyad]] clan massacre by the [[event:2133|Abbasid]] revolutionaries in 750 AD — who escaped to North Africa and then crossed to Spain, where he overthrew the Abbasid governor and established an independent Muslim state refusing to recognize the Abbasid Caliphate's authority. This created the political divide between the eastern (Baghdad-centered) and western (Cordoba-centered) Islamic worlds that would persist for centuries.

Abd al-Rahman I's achievement was extraordinary: alone, with no army, pursued by Abbasid agents, he built from scratch a state that within decades was one of the most sophisticated in Europe. He began construction of the [[event:2193|Great Mosque of Cordoba]] (785 AD), established a professional army of loyal Berber and slave soldiers (avoiding dependence on Arab tribal politics), and created an administrative system drawing on Umayyad Damascus traditions. The emirate reached its peak under Abd al-Rahman III, who in 929 AD elevated it to a caliphate ([[event:2192|Cordoba Caliphate]]), claiming the title of Commander of the Faithful in direct challenge to the Abbasids. The cultural achievement of Umayyad al-Andalus — its philosophy, poetry, medicine, architecture, and agricultural innovation — made it the most advanced European civilization of the early medieval period.
